blocks.text: At the lowest level, everything a computer does comes down to a CPU fetching instructions from memory, decoding them, and executing them — billions of times per second. Understanding this cycle unlocks how programs actually run.
The CPU continuously runs through three steps for every instruction:

items.title: 1. Fetch
items.description: The Control Unit reads the next instruction from memory (at the address stored in the Program Counter register). The PC then increments to point to the next instruction.

items.title: 2. Decode
items.description: The instruction is decoded — the Control Unit figures out what operation to perform (ADD, LOAD, STORE, JUMP, etc.) and which operands (data values or addresses) are involved.

items.title: 3. Execute
items.description: The ALU carries out the operation. The result is written to a register or back to memory. If it's a JUMP instruction, the Program Counter is updated to a new address.

blocks.title: Clock Speed
blocks.text: The CPU is synchronized by a **clock signal** that ticks billions of times per second (GHz). Each tick can advance the pipeline by one step. A 3 GHz CPU performs roughly 3 billion cycles per second.
Accessing data from different types of memory takes very different amounts of time. The CPU is designed to leverage faster (and smaller) memory as much as possible:

blocks.title: Why This Matters for Programmers
blocks.text: Accessing RAM is ~100× slower than L1 cache. Writing code that uses **cache-friendly** data access patterns (sequential array access instead of random pointer chasing) can make programs dramatically faster.
When you press the power button, a carefully orchestrated sequence loads the operating system from storage into RAM:

items: **Power On** — Electrical power reaches the motherboard
**BIOS/UEFI** — Firmware stored in a ROM chip runs first, performs POST (Power-On Self Test) to check hardware
**Boot Loader Found** — BIOS locates the boot device (SSD) and loads the bootloader (GRUB, Windows Boot Manager)
**OS Kernel Loaded** — The bootloader loads the OS kernel into RAM
**Kernel Initializes** — Sets up memory management, device drivers, file systems
**User Space Starts** — The OS launches system services and the login interface

blocks.text: How Programs Are Loaded and Run
When you double-click an application, the OS performs several steps before your first line of code executes:

items.title: 1. File Read — OS reads the executable from disk
items.content: The OS reads the binary file (EXE, ELF, Mach-O) from storage. This file contains the machine code instructions plus metadata about how to load it.

items.title: 2. Memory Allocation — OS allocates address space
items.content: The OS creates a new process and allocates virtual memory regions: code segment (instructions), data segment (global variables), stack (local variables, function calls), and heap (dynamic memory).

items.title: 3. Linking — Dynamic libraries are connected
items.content: The dynamic linker connects your program to shared libraries (DLLs on Windows, .so on Linux). This lets many programs share the same copy of common code (like the C runtime library).

items.title: 4. CPU Starts Executing
items.content: The OS sets the Program Counter to the entry point of your program (typically main()). The fetch-decode-execute cycle begins, and your program runs.

points: The CPU runs the Fetch-Decode-Execute cycle for every instruction
Cache memory dramatically reduces the time the CPU spends waiting for data
The memory hierarchy trades speed for capacity at each level
The boot process goes: power on → BIOS → bootloader → kernel → user space
Running a program involves: read from disk → allocate memory → link libraries → execute

questions.question: What happens when you press the power button on a computer?
questions.answer: Power reaches the motherboard. The BIOS/UEFI firmware runs, performs POST (hardware self-test), then locates the bootloader on the boot device. The bootloader loads the OS kernel into RAM. The kernel initializes hardware drivers, memory management, and file systems. Finally, user-space processes (login screen, desktop) are launched.

questions.question: Why is cache memory important for CPU performance?
questions.answer: RAM access takes ~100 nanoseconds, while L1 cache takes ~1 ns. Without cache, the CPU would sit idle 99% of the time waiting for data. Cache stores recently used data closer to the CPU cores. The hit rate (fraction of accesses served from cache) dramatically impacts overall performance — modern CPUs have multi-level caches (L1/L2/L3) to maximize hits.
